comments.title: Chapter 3 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on Memory
comments.content:
Good Evening,

Where I am, we've gotten some much needed rain and very welcome cooler temperatures, so I hope everyone here has some similar welcome fall weather.

To Share: I've put out the next installment of my review of Maria Kvilhaug's /The Seed of Yggdrasill/, this time on the "Maiden with the Mead": https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_the_seed_of_yggdrasill_3_the_maiden_with_the_mead

To Ask: The heathen lore puts a lot of emphasis on "memory" (Odin's Raven Muninn, The Mead of Memory, Mimir and Hoenir, and lots more). Do you, JMG, or anyone else here, know of any other spiritual traditions that seem to put a lot of emphasis on "memory" as one of the most important spiritual qualities/abilities to be sought for and developed? For what it's worth, I get the impression that in the heathen context, it seems to be linked with remembering your individuality/true self, rather than over-identifying with your personality, but I might be over-reading into things.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings and best wishes to all who will have them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Chapter 3 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on Memory
replies.content: Renaissance Hermeticism put a great deal of attention into the development of memory through the ars memorativa or Art of Memory. That was partly because practice of the Art was a great way to develop concentration and imagination, but having a clear, accurate, and well-stocked memory was considered to be an important asset to Hermetic spirituality.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 3 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on Memory
replies.content: Are there books or techniques you recommend to train the memory?

replies.title: Re: Chapter 3 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on Memory
replies.content: Er, I just named the technique -- the ars memorativa or Art of Memory. Do a search on either of those phrases and you'll get plenty of resources.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 3 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on Memory
replies.content:
Hello,
I imagine that almost any Spiritual tradition that is older than the advent of printing must have placed a very high importance on the ability to precisely memorize. Especially before the widespread phenomenon of literacy, many teachings were to be encoded into stories. I think this is because humans are particularly adapted to this form of learning.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 3 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on Memory
replies.content: Thank you! Memory does seem to be very tightly linked with poetry and storytelling in the Norse lore, so it wouldn't surprise me if that's a widespread theme.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 3 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on Memory
replies.content:
This isn't specifically spiritual but I'm constantly aggravated by people who think a pronouncable acronym is a mnemonic.

I did an Open University course on systems thinking. If I remember rightly the soft systems methodology had an acronym CATWOE. I will remember that word until my dying day, but have no idea what a single letter of it meant.

To work properly, a mnemonic needs to work on several levels, an may need to be individualised for how each person's mind works. These are some that work for me:

The difference between a stalagmite and stalagtite. A stalagtite hangs from the ceiling, so gravity pulls it 'tight'.

Bactrian camel and dromedary. Take a capital B and capital D and tip them onto their backs. B has two humps, D has one.

Ionic columns have spirals at the top that look like 'eyes'. With that one as the anchor, it's easy enough to remember Doric are plain, Corinthian are leaves, and Composite are leaves and eyes. (Just looking this up now to make sure I was remembering it right, I found there's also Tuscan, which is like Doric but with a smooth column rather than fluted. Also I thought the leaves on the Corinthian were palm fronds, but they're actually acanthus.)

replies.title: Re: Chapter 3 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on Memory
replies.content:
I got curious and looked up CATWOE. It stands for 'Customer, Actor, Transformation, Worldview, Owner and environment', in other words the usual pretentious utterly forgettable business gobble-de-gook. I'm not surprised you couldn't remember what it stood for. It's enough to make any cat wail in anguish.

JLfromNH/Azure Officious Shoggoth

replies.title: Re: Chapter 3 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on Memory
replies.content:
Those are pretty good! I think you're right that an acronym is only a building block for a mnemonic, not an answer in-and-of-itself. For me, it's roughly equivalent to memorizing a sequence/order - sometimes knowing what 1 and 3 are forces me to think about what 2 could be, and then I get it. Of course, much of that might be personal, because I was in the Army and went to Business School after, so I've had a lot of exposure to acronyms!

My own personal favorite mnemonic was one I worked out when I was learning the bones of the human body for high school biology. I couldn't remember which of the bones in the forearm was the radius, and which the ulna, so I started wiggling my thumb whenever I thought of "radius" and my pinky whenever I thought of "ulna." To this day, that's the only way I remember it (I had to confirm just now by taking my fingers off the keyboard and wiggling them). I suspect there's a lot that could be done with such "kinesthetic mnemonics," but in the past when I've tried to find examples, the best I've gotten are certain traditional dances, or one guy who told me his First Nations grandmother associated different stories with different bones in her body.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Chapter 3 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on Memory
replies.content:
Jeff, I think you are not referring so much to 'memorization' such as Druids, bards of all cultures and so forth would have practiced, but rather to the exploration of one's personal and transpersonal memories--is that right?

We have the saying of Odin, referring to his Ravens who gather news from the Worlds for him each day, where he says he fears for the loss of Huginn (Thinking), but more for the loss of Muninn (contemplative memory). What is the deepest meaning of what he is saying here? (A good seed for discursive contemplation with Odin!)

Historically, I think much of the Heathen emphasis on memory is based on how much they valued personal fame and reputation, how important it was for them and their deeds to be remembered after death.

In respect to your question, we can look at a culture with similar ideas--that of ancient Greece. Mnemosyne, the Goddess of Memory, is a Titaness of the highest birth, the daughter of Ouranos and Gaea, Sky / Heavens and Earth. After nine nights with Zeus she gives birth to the nine Muses. In the earliest attested practice, there was a festival held for the Muses every 4 years, called the Museia, whose gifts and talents all depend on, and perpetuate, memories. 'Music' and 'museum' are named after them, and both involve memory. A museum stores cultural memory, and setting words to music is the most powerful way to memorize those words.

I think that when we consider the importance of memory in ancient cultures, we should not ignore the aspects of cultural and historical memory, which function to make a community and culture 'live' through ages of time, not be fleeting and ephemeral. The memories of individuals and their deeds are imbedded in cultural memory and gain much of their value and strength thereby. Indeed, the value of their deeds comes from their contributions to their community and culture.

This may well tie in with what you're saying, Jeff, in a metaphysical sort of way. We can see 'personality' as the individual living person, concerned with the survival of their own fame and deeds. We can see the memory of a person and their deeds / contributions as an individual imbedded in a culture, a 'higher' expression of the person in a larger context, though not as high as the 'true self.' But a step along the way, moving from the personal towards the transpersonal.

As far as remembering one's true self in Heathen thought, I think Odin holds the key. He's involved with all the 'memory' symbols--Raven, Mead, Mimir and his Well, poetry, prophecy, runes, and more. What was he seeking when he hung on the Tree, a sacrifice of 'himself to himSelf?' Runes, yes, but this 'sacrifice of self to self' implies something along the lines of what you're saying, seeking wisdom from some higher aspect of himself.

Perhaps you might ask him about this yourself! :-)

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Chapter 3 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on Memory
replies.content:
Thank you for this! You are right about where I think I'm more going with this line of exploration, though I am open to the possibility that things like the Ars Memorativa or stuffing tons of poetry into your head may have more to do with it than it might first seem.

Embarrassingly enough, I hadn't thought much about the fame/reputation side of things yet - how you are remembered, rather than what you yourself can remember, along with the implications you spell out for cultural memory. You're right that will make for a fine thread for meditation!

Thank you for the suggestion to look into Mnemosyne and the muses - for a former classics student, I sometimes don't go as readily to the Greek myths as I ought. I wonder if the Pythagoreans, with their practice of remembering the day in reverse, had any particular devotion to Mnemosyne or the Muses?

Lastly, you have indeed gotten to the heart of the matter - the main reason for this particular line of inquiry is that I am working on the "All Father" prayer for my Heathen Rosary, and that has turned into a deep dive on all things Odinic. So far, when I've asked, I've gotten a response something like "I've given you the tools, now do the work!" I think the research, meditation, and discussion is itself a form of devotion that's getting me to where I need to be to write the poem.

As always, thanks very much for your insights,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The aptitude for clairvoyance does run in some families. Occult tradition has it that this was the result of deliberate breeding programs in the civilizations during the last ice age, which are remembered today only via the myths of Atlantis; the reason the Second Sight is so common among Celtic peoples is that northwestern Europe received many refugees from the Sacred Clan, the group produced by these programs, when the oceans rose after 9600 BC.

That's not the same thing as what you're asking about, though. Magic, as Dion Fortune defined it, is the art and science of causing change in consciousness in accordance with will. Everyone practices it all the time; the problem is that most people do it unconsciously and incompetently, and so make a mess of their lives. Do some people inadvertently cast evil spells? Of course, though it's not all that inadvertent; when you project anger and hatred at someone and wish that something bad would happen to them, you're casting a curse on them more or less deliberately, even if you pretend to yourself that it won't have any effect.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I believe this "Psychic Celt Gene" theory needs some revisiting. The archeological and genetic evidence we have available today seems to directly contradict any notion of direct population continuity in the British Isles from the Stone Age all the way up to today. We know now from genetic studies that around 2450 BC there was a massive population replacement event that forever changed the Isles' genetic makeup. A group of "Bell Beaker" migrants of Central European origin (via the Lower Rhine area) landed on the Isles and within just a century or two, almost completely replaced their Neolithic predecessors, i.e. the builders of Stonehenge and other Megalithic sites. This migration event is why the R1b Y-haplogroup (paternal lineage marker) is so dominant among the men (present company included, haha), especially those from Ireland, Scotland, Wales, and the Western parts of England. Of course what language or type of language these newcomers actually spoke will always be a mystery, since they didn't write anything down. It's likely that Celtic speech didn't arrive on the Isles until around 500 BC, probably from Gaul to both Britain and Ireland, and later in another wave from Iberia to Ireland around 100-50 BC or so.

Interesting source on the above:
https://archaeology.co.uk/articles/features/prehistoric-pop-culture-deciphering-the-dna-of-the-bell-beaker-complex.htm

Of course none of this is to say that the peoples of Ireland, Wales, and other Celtic areas of the Isles (at least around the time Dion Fortune first theorized this), were not more predisposed to having latent psychic perception and abilities than other Europeans. That is probably true based on qualitative observation (plus the old British habit of trying to differentiate themselves as much as possible from their Irish neighbors), but I think there can be one or more alternative hypotheses which can better explain why this is so. I can think of a few off the top of my head (like the mysterious origins of the Druids having something to do with this, or magical technologies the Neolithic peoples implanted into the land, ect.), but I don't want to make this comment any longer than it already is.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The scholarly consensus on this veers back and forth from the invasion-and-replacement theory to the continuity theory. Right now the invasion-and-replacement theory is more popular. Yet there's also genetic and archeological evidence for considerable continuity --

https://www.independent.co.uk/news/the-family-link-that-reaches-back-300-generations-to-a-cheddar-cave-1271542.html

I suspect that when the dust finally settles, it'll turn out that there's been repeated replacements of patrilines, and a great deal of continuity in matrilines. Invading armies very often slaughter the men, but very rarely do the same with the women -- and for what it's worth, most of the people I know who have the second sight and Celtic ancestry trace their psychic gifts via their mothers...

Dear JMG and commentariat,

I do not know, if foreseeing the future was common among the Slavs-even though the few legends I learned at school might indicate it used to be so, but for the present, Czech mainstream society does not even deem it possible.

Funnily enough, I have had a strange foreseeing-the-future-gift since childhood, but it brought a lot of confusion caused especially with the strong emotions connected with the “this is impossible but somehow I am doing it”. I have never cultivated the gift and during the last two decades tried to let it “sleep”, but one never knows when it announces one or another future event…

One of the periods of my life when I was trying to be “as normal as possible” (fortunately, I am not doing that any more-in my current worldview nobody is fully normal :o)) was around the election of a certain American president. I was not very interested; everybody predicted it would be Hillary and I did not give it a second thought. Night before the election, I was teaching, using picture cards of different characters for practicing questions like “What’s his name? How old is he? Etc.”. Little tired, not paying too much attention. Suddenly, an uncharacteristically loud voice of my thoughts said: “The new president will be Trump!”. I remember thinking something like “Hey, this is in no way foreseeing, since the probability is about 50%” just before a flash of a brief vision of a particular picture card came. A moment later, the student drew the one picture card out of many…there I was for the probability cheeky talk!

Once I was telling this story to my friend – the one who later lent me some of your books, and she surprised me with: “Well, I knew it would be Trump in advance, too.” I got excited for I have never heard her speaking of foreseeing the future. She smiled and said: “You know, I am reading JMG’s blog. I read the info there.” :o)

Have a nice day,
Markéta

replies.content:
Have you considered trying to nurture that gift, as it is quite a profound one? There are plenty of people who have some extra aptitude in the weak but always existent human capability of perceiving currents and energy flows in the dimension of fate. Most folks with that particular extra gift get its use completely scared out of them, but that talent can also be cultivated back with a little bit of effort. Around what aspects of fate does your gift tend to offer up glimpses? Is it mostly of grand historical events or of little daily opportunities, only of importance to your life?

Fate is intrinsically tied to the power of will, given that our limited human understanding of fate pretty much works out to be the direction that all the conflicting wills in the universe, massed together, will eventually end up heading in. That's one reason you don't want to piss off a god, as their wills are so much more potent than ours that just one truly committed adversary in the larger pantheon can easily send you spiraling off in a direction you never ever would have willfully intended. Of course, befriending gods can lead to the opposite effect of amplifying your will well beyond its human limitations.

Anyway, if you're interested in cultivating your gift of sensing the currents of fate, playing around with how you apply your own willpower is a very good place to start. Even more highly than that, I recommend asking the gods for their help in developing your gift in whatever direction you're interested in taking it. Or better yet, in whatever direction they think would be best for you to go in. After all, the gods can sense your fate far more clearly than any human, no matter how gifted, could ever hope to.

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Sphare of Protection and spiritual alchemy
replies.content:
Yes, and in fact this is a very good habit. I recommend practicing some kind of basic protective ritual every single day, whether or not you notice anything that has to be banished. The version of the Sphere of Protection taught in the Modern Order of Essenes is certainly one of the options; if you're interested in some of the others, the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose papers, vol. 1 pages 10-25 and 40-41, covers several options. You can download the FHR papers free of charge here:

https://octagonsociety.org/archives/fhr/

comments.title: The Atlantean Prohibition
comments.content:
I've been reflecting on the discussions here and on the other blog about an apparent prohibition on certain types of technology during times of deep materialism. More specifically, the apparent prohibition on certain types of etheric technology and associated magic that has been used for nefarious purposes in the past. I have wondered why, say, in Lemurian times, various higher powers didn't intervene, the way they may be currently. It occurred to me that at the time of the lemurian deviation, there would have been very few advanced human souls who would have been around to care, understand, and act. However, when certain etheric technologies and polarity magic got abused in later cycles, for instance, by the Atlanteans, there would have been a few more advanced souls around, but still not enough to really do much, with the disasters of that age having been probably caused by a misuse of a particular technology (which hasn't been fully lost either, but that's another story).

Roll forward to the next danger period, after the renaissance when a combination of material science, magical understanding, and a gross energy and resource surplus could have easily seen a second lemurian deviation or another mistake of that nature, and there would have been a lot more advanced souls around to bring about and support Protestantism on the inner planes, thus removing some of the risk.

It's actually pretty elegant, but it isn't over yet, and requires a certain patience from occultists.

replies.title: Re: The Atlantean Prohibition
replies.content: That's an intriguing speculation.

comments.title: Psionic
comments.content:
Hello JMG

I am interested in the topic of Psionics, Is it just another name for magic (more Sci-fi) Is it a separate branch of spiritual/psychic development? Are there any books or materials that you (or anyone else) could recommend on this topic? (Things like telekinesis, extrasensory vision).

replies.title: Re: Psionic
replies.content:
I think the term originates from mid 20th century Sci-fi as a catch all for telepathy, telekinesis, clairvoyance etc. There are all sorts of books, some dubious, touting the development of such abilities but it might be wise to look at Patanjali's Yoga Sutras and similar works. They're a bit of slog to go through but talk about the appearance of siddhis (what we might now call psionics) as a sign the meditator has reached a certain level of spiritual development. But they also strongly warn against getting caught up in them as they can derail the meditator from his or her path. Ego-inflation is the least of the problems which could crop up (I've got superpowers! Yowza!!)

While I'm guessing samadhi is not your ultimate goal, it might be well to heed the warnings and go slowly. As with kundalini, it's very easy to get yourself into a hole and not be able to get out.

JLfromNH/Azure Officious Shoggoth

replies.title: Re: Psionic
replies.content:
Psionics! Now there's a blast from the past. The term was as far as I know invented by John Campbell, the editor of Astounding Science Fiction Stories, around 1950. Those were the days when science fiction writers and readers genuinely believed that they were blazing new trails for humanity into the future, and Campbell in particular felt that the hidden powers of the mind and soul had to be included in that -- it wasn't until the early 1980s that science fiction fell into the grip of the dogmatic materialists and all the more interesting ideas in the genre got thrown out.

There were some very interesting movements toward developing a science of psionics, notably by the people associated with Borderland Sciences Research Foundation (BSRF), but all of it's long out of print and hard to find -- if you look up "psionics" these days you'll find a godzillion books on roleplaying games. Terms less cluttered up with irrelevancies are "radionics" and "radiesthesia," and you can also see what you can find via BSRF's website at https://borderlandsciences.org/ .

This isn't a request for either medical information, or for ritual magic around a child, but do you or anyone else know of any folk/natural magic remedies for ensuring a good night's sleep for a young child (less than 3 years old) - I'm thinking of herbs to hang in the bedroom, pictures, that kind of thing.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One thing I find helps when the energy in a bedroom (or anyone other room) is a bit "stressy" is to simply clean the room-- both physically and astrally. To clean it astrally I simple clap out the room, I mean, open the windows, go in there and clap hands, clap in the middle of the room, clap up towards the ceiling, clap down to the floor, clap in the corners, clap under bed. Then, close the windows, and standing in the center with hands together over the heart, send out intentions of peace and blessings to fill the space. This is a quick way of breaking up astral crud that might be disturbing, and also making the space less attractive to astral negs which can disturb some children.

If the child likes lavender, it might help to finish the clap-out with spray a bit of lavender in the room. (Just use a spray bottle, fill with water, add a few drops of lavender oil.) It's a strong scent, though. I like it and find it very calming, but not everyone does.

Depends on the child but sometimes keeping a dim nightlight on, rather than leaving the room totally dark, can also be calming and help them get to sleep.

Also, depends on the child, but the ritual of getting into bed and then having a parent read a charming (not too exciting) story from a book can also be very helpful.

P.S. I would advise strongly against keeping / allowing any kind of electronics (such as baby monitor or iPad) in the room.


SDPM

replies.title: sleepy child
replies.content:
Here's one possibility to help your child sleep. In the past when my fingers were less arthritic, I braided 'blessing cords' for many purposes, including for blessing and protecting babies, and they seemed to work well.

The more braiding that goes into the cord, the more effective it seems to be, so I usually used either 9 or 27 strands, but 3 can also be used. Choose some embroidery threads in gentle, soothing colors, whatever you think would be helpful. Measure the strands to be a little longer than the child's height.

Begin with 3 strands at a time. Tie a loose knot a couple inches above one end of the threads, then tie those ends loosely to some item of furniture to hold the strands straight while you braid them.

As you braid, sing lullabies or other lovely, soothing songs. Recite blessings for the child. Envision and describe beautiful scenery that promotes restfulness and serenity. Call for blessings from loving ancestors. recite little bedtime stories. Make up little sleepy chants and hums. Keep your mind, spirit and voice active on such themes the whole time you are braiding.

If you're making a three-stranded braid, then you're done now. Untie the end of the braid from its anchor, tie a knot near the untied end of the braid, and trim the ends of both fringes.

If you're making a 9-strander then continue making 3 braids this way (you don't have to do them all at the same time, you can spread this out as needed). Then follow the same procedure of singing etc. while braiding these 3 braids together, giving you a 9-stranded braid. If you want a 27-strander, then keep going the same way!

When you're done with the whole thing, carefully untie the ends of each individual braid, then tie all the braids into a knot at each end, leaving an inch or two of fringe, which you can trim. (If you leave all the knots in the individual braids, it makes a horrible lumpy knot when you try to tie everything together.)

Wash the completed braid in cold salt-water, squeeze it out in a towel, then lay it to dry--outdoors if you can. The final challenge is to *securely* wrap and tie the braid somewhere close to the child's bed in a place / way that is safe from the child being able to untie it and get tangled up in it. On the bedstead or a bedside lamp is a possibility, but if your child is the Houdini-type, perhaps the most secure place is somewhere under the bed--tied onto the box-springs, or placed between mattress and box-springs or bed platform. Obviously, do this when the child is not watching!

When putting the child to bed, repeat some of the songs, chants, hums, stories, blessings that you used when making the cord, to amplify the cord and to join yourself and your child together in a moment of peace and blessing (you can do this even if the child is fussing, just keep your focus anyway; peacefulness can be contagious!)

I hope this might be helpful for you; if nothing else, all this soothing activity will soothe your own frazzled nerves! :-)

Many blessings,
Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: sleepy child

replies.title: Re: sleepy child
replies.content:
Jeff, this is mostly my own rendition of braiding charms; I know it’s a traditional practice but don’t recall seeing any specific instructions about how to do it. (Knots, charms, amulets, herbal magic, etc were called lybesn inAnglo-Saxon, and the woman who made them was called a lybbestre, FYI.)

I do especially love the complex 27-strand braids, where you first braid 3 sets of 3 strands each, then braid those 3 together, then make 2 more of those 3X3 braids, then braid all three 3X3s together for the 27 strands. If you choose interesting colors, the complexity of the interwoven colors is quite fascinating. If you want to make this one a specific length you need to start with threads that are considerably longer than you need, because all the braiding shortens the length by quite a bit.

The singing, chanting, humming, reciting, praying and speaking can be quite demanding as you work on the more complex braids. You can try doing all of that spontaneously or from memory, or you can prepare at least some of it in advance. You can also use songbooks, poetry or storybooks, or cheat sheets to read from if you need to, just lay them out so you can see them while you braid. It’s okay to repeat yourself!

One thing I forgot to mention is to rinse the cord after washing with saltwater. The salt not only cleanses, but also helps to keep the dyes from running. My preferred brand of embroidery thread is DMC cotton perle.

I recommend to students of Heathen soul lore to make such cords as soul-tokens to work with during practice. You can see my article on soul-tokens on my website.

I hope you enjoy getting into this!
Winifred

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hmm. I think that really depends on the child. In many cases, the most important magical ingredients are probably endurance and the will to understand why your child doesn't find sleep. Sometimes it's very little things that are wrong (like total darkness, against which many children have a strong aversion, or some other discomfort). But sometimes the cause might lie in the being of the child and there's little you can do about it except patiently being there for the child and carry the burden. In those cases using natural magic to support yourself to stay patient and steady (or to gain the clarity to understand what's going on) might be very valuable. It's really a very wide spectrum, which also strongly depends on the age of the child.

In my (of course limited) experience - if you can recall the birth and especially the first few minutes in the life of your child, you might find very valuable clues to better understand your child.

One warning, if I may, which is based on observations I made with the children of close relatives: Never do anything to your child that you couldn't stand yourself. For example: Don't force it to listen to music for excessive amounts of time (which is easily ensured if you use your own voice but not an electronic device), don't force it to experience motion for excessive amounts of time or with excessive force or amplitude (which is easily ensured if you use your own muscles but not some motorized device), and so on. Of course, not every motorized cradle or every piece of music played from an electronic device are devils work, far from it. But there are risks involved and in the cases I know, I can witness the damage it caused almost every day. Some is minor, but some far from it.

All the best for you and your child!
Nachtgurke

comments.title: AI Fortune Tellers

Good morning. I apologize if I'm late to the party and this topic has already been addressed. But it recently came to my attention that AI fortune tellers are now a thing on the Internet. Do you think this is a bad idea? I think it's a horrible, potentially disastrous, idea.

At the very least, people will be seeking life advice from something that mimics a human being and gives them a simulacrum of human interaction without any real empathy. At the worst, I imagine not-so-nice entities could get their little claws into these programs and really play merry heck with people's lives.

Thoughts?

replies.title: Re: AI Fortune Tellers
replies.content:
This isn't even remotely new, though slapping the AI brand on it is a bit of clever marketing. You could get your fortune told by mechanical fortune tellers before electronics were even invented. Yes, it's a bad idea, precisely because the machines don't have empathy and aren't capable of intuition, so the results are no more accurate than pure chance -- and thus much less accurate than a competent diviner.

Yes, it's possible that malign spirits could get involved, but then that's common enough anyway. My more serious concern is that the machines might be programmed to adapt their predictions to what the listeners want to hear, at which point you have a positive-feedback loop amplifying bad ideas out past the point of absurdity and quite possibly all the way to catastrophe.

replies.title: Re: AI Fortune Tellers

Hi JMG,

Something occurred to me regarding psychedelics, and that is that, while Neptune rules drugs in general, the psychedelic influence on Western culture seems to coincide with the rise of Plutonian influence. For example, one of the first Westerners to study mescaline was Havelock Ellis in the 1890s, around the same time that psychoanalysis was first emerging; experiments with mescaline became more prevalent during the 20s and 30s, as nuclear energy was really taking off; Albert Hoffman discovered the effects of LSD in 1943, two years before the first atom bomb went off.

Furthermore, psychedelics in Western culture were almost immediately taken up by psychoanalysts and therapists, on the one side, and intelligence agents on the other, looking for methods to 'break open' - or split - the human psyche. The notion of 'integration' of the psychedelic experience is popular among advocates today, suggesting that the experience itself has a fragmenting, or splitting, effect. (Incidentally, a popular book on psychedelics was titled Breaking Open the Head).

Finally, like much of the phenomena associated with Pluto's influence, psychedelics have proved to be very much overhyped - every generation since the 1950s has seen some advocates claiming that some world-shattering change is imminent due to their influence, but it's the same old story, over and over again.

I wonder, then, once 2036 comes around, if their influence will wane to a small section of enthusiasts, while the rest of the world shrugs and moves on to other concerns.

Luke

comments.title: Oaths
comments.content:
Oaths

Last week there was a discussion of oaths and their consequences when broken. I can speak to that a bit, since in my religion of Heathenry it was an important concept and practice in the past, and for serious Heathens it is still so today.

JMG explained that the bad luck resulting from failed or broken oaths was generated by collective faith in the religious powers being invoked by the oath (such as swearing on the Bible for Christians), and as this faith faded into mere convention, the power that ‘punished’ forsworn oaths also faded.

I would certainly agree with this, but add another dimension that comes from our modern Heathen interpretations of ancient practices. This is the idea that when one swears a formal oath, one is actually standing at the Well of Wyrd. The words and intent of the oath are not only heard and marked by the Gods and Goddesses, but also by the Norns. Crucially, the words fall or are placed into the Well itself, and thus inescapably shape one’s wyrd / orlog henceforward.

Not only that, but others involved in the oath or affected by it may also be wrapped into the orlog resulting from the oath. Some Heathens are very cautious about swearing oaths or even being in the presence of a formal oath being sworn, fearing that if the oath fails or is forsworn, the wyrd of all present will be adversely affected. By the same token, some will recruit an ‘oath-helper’ to join in their oath, to support their commitment, keep them on track, and reduce the likelihood of failure.

An oath may be formally challenged when it is spoken, if Heathen elders present believe it is unrealistic, and the oath-taker is urged to either defend their ability to achieve the oath, or reconsider, re-word, modify, or withdraw the oath. The oath-taker may also be asked to agree to a shild commensurate with the impact of the oath they are swearing. Shild is a formally stated debt, a penalty that must be paid if the oath fails or is forsworn, even if this is due to circumstances beyond one’s control. Usually the shild takes the form of a service, a charitable donation, or personal sacrifice for the well-being of Heathenry, but it can take any form of ‘coin or kind.’

There still end up being people who not only fail their oaths, but also fail to pay their resulting shild, with no effort to make up for these failures in some other way. They simply fail to take these matters seriously. The penalty then is an obvious loss of respect from the Heathen community, as well as resentment from others who were present at the oath-taking or affected by it, and feel there has been a negative impact on their own orlog / wyrd thereby.

I am concerned about a trend toward over-cautiousness, because it results in pressure for Heathens not to take formal, publicly recognized oaths at all. I and others feel that successful oath-taking is an irreplaceable method of growing ‘might and main,’ soul-power and strength of character, earning respect from our fellows and from the Deities, and laying layers of orlog in the Well that will benefit ourselves and others around us. Not to mention the specific benefits of whatever we are swearing to achieve.

Heathen oath-taking is fraught with challenges, pitfalls, and great benefits. It is a unique way of developing strength of character, strengthening our human and divine relationships, and shaping our life in powerful ways. I’ve written two articles to support this practice, available on my website HeathenSoulLore.net if anyone would like to know more about this. One is “Oaths: What they mean and why they matter;” the other is “The Practice of Heathen Oathing.”

Bottom line: at least in a few little corners of the world, oaths are still practiced and honored as they should be!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Mercury - Air in John Gilbert's teachings
replies.content:
Hmm. The material was part of the FHR teachings, but it requires a little familiarity with the Tree of Life to decode. The 8th sphere of the Tree corresponds to Mercury, and in John's system (though not in most others) that sphere is assigned to Air. The planets correspond to the elements in his system in the following way:

Neptune - Air
Uranus - Fire
Saturn - Water
Jupiter - Water
Mars - Fire
Sun - Air
Venus - Fire
Mercury - Air
Moon - Water

The element of earth is always and only, in his system, assigned to the Earth. You can find all this in the following post:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/175431.html

comments.title: Another Magic Monday
comments.content:
Hi JMG and the rest,

1. I wish I had been more active on this forum recently, but I have been hit by some pretty heavy karma lately. My mother was diagnosed with a life-threatening illness and my father's health is not good either. They are not young. Luckily, I am in the area and can help out, and they helped me out earlier in life, so I feel that it is my duty right now to pay it forward. It is a pretty sad time. My question is, how do you advise to act with regard to spiritual pursuits in this situation? Will they be beneficial in a time like this?

Divinations were mixed, and seem to suggest that I had stopped making progress (10 of Swords), and would have to balance my life (2 of Pentacles), and start over (Page of Wands), if I'm reading this correctly. A more specific question about meditation and protection suggested yes (2 of Wands). If I could accomplish all that, the outcome would be success, it seems (6 of Wands), but I imagine this will not be easy at all.

2. Things like this make you re-evaluate things, and one thing that happened unexpectedly is that last night I thought back to people now departed who had been a part of my life in the past: Grandparents, a great aunt, an uncle, and even my friend's father. Some have been gone for some time. I felt the urge to say a prayer to each of their memories with some specific thoughts about what they did in life, how I hope they find peace and reflection in between lives, and that I hope the gods guide them to what they need in the next life. After finishing, I felt a strong surge of emotion, and I am not a very emotional person by character (water is my weakest element). Would you take this as a sign that the prayer was heard?

3. More proactively I am thinking that I have not always been the best friend or family member. I almost feel like sending blessings to friends and family or at least a note of gratitude, but it's so out of character that I think people might take it strangely, except given the circumstances maybe they would understand. Since this is tangentially related to spiritual questions (blessing or healing in a way), I wondered if you have an opinion on whether that's worthwhile or not?

And a comment:

4. On a previous Magic Monday, there was a discussion inspired by Vine Deloria about ties to the land and "becoming indigenous". JMG's comment was that his forefathers had settled Puget Sound generations ago, and that this was a place that felt like home in a way nowhere else did, but the conditions of our collective life today made it necessary for him to wander far afield. On reflection, I realized I felt a similar tie to the woods and lakes of Central Minnesota where my mom grew up. It was like a home away from home, and my ancestors first settled there in the late 19th century, when it was a wilderness roamed by Ojibwe, themselves wanderers from the eastern USA in an earlier time. This summer my uncle sold my grandparents' property and land, since he has since some time migrated elsewhere (ironically, to the area JMG grew up), so I will be severed from that old place for a time. That added to the sense of loss this summer. Just wanted to say that this discussion was the inspiration for a very moving meditation on the importance of place. The Faustian world has been semi-nomadic for a long time and I think there's a longing for the restlessness to cease.

Best to you all,

Deneb

replies.title: Re: Another Magic Monday
replies.content:
1) In difficult times it's especially important to maintain at least basic spiritual practices -- in the occult traditions I teach, that amounts to a daily protective ritual, a daily meditation, and a daily divination. Those are a source of strength and balance in trying times, and they guarantee that whatever happens, you'll still be on the Path.

2) I'd take it as an answer, yes.

3) I'd encourage you to send the notes of gratitude, and explain in them that it's because of what's going on with your parents. That ought to help them understand.

4) Very likely, yes.

I have a few questions about Druidry that I hope you can please help me with.

1. Are there advantages to working with pantheons of deities from your own country/culture? For example, would an Irish person living in Ireland get better results working with Irish deities than with other pantheons? 

I'm guessing that genetic/ancestral connections along with connections to sacred spaces in a given country would be beneficial, but not necessary. It goes without saying that anybody can work with whatever pantheon(s) they choose to work with.

2. I've often heard that deities sometimes express interest in working with people. Is that true? Does it take years of working with a pantheon in order to know if you have a resonance with it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That depends very much on the individual. Some people find this to be true, others emphatically do not.

2) Yes, sometimes deities take the initiative; very often it's because the person had a connection to that deity in a previous incarnation. As for how long it takes, again, it varies with the individual.

3) If you don't have an extensive knowledge of esotericism and haven't learned the lessons you learn by making the implements, I don't recommend doing godform work! What I'd recommend instead is, first, to read plenty of mythologies and see which ones resonate with you; and second, when you find a mythology that has a strong appeal, try praying to whichever of the deities appeals to you most, and see what response you get.

replies.title: (no subject)

To add to 1): Definitely follow JMG's advice and find myths that resonate with you. I'd also think about the historical context of the pantheons in question. The gods have their own motivations, but humanity is an active participant in our joint relationship.

A lot of religious history tends to look at gods as human creation, but if you squint hard enough and compare with the myths/texts themselves you could reinterpret these histories with the gods as active participants.

The cool thing about doing this is you could then look at our own time, see the modern religious traditions around us, and place your own practice in that context.

replies.title: Re: Anxiety and Panic
replies.content:
First, I would strongly encourage you to talk to your homeopath, as panic fears are a known symptom that is often treated with homeopathic medicine. Second, I'd encourage you to do some serious journaling. Very often panic fears like this are covering up for some other, deeper issue, and journaling can help you come to grips with that deeper issue. Write "What am I frightened of?" and then write down whatever comes to mind, no matter how irrelevant it seems to be. Let it flow freely. When you run out of things to write, come up with another question, write that, and see what you can get. Among other things, this process lets the pressure of repressed emotions bleed off, making it easier to deal with life.

You might also consider doing the exercises of the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, which are all about releasing old emotions. You can find them here:

https://octagonsociety.org/preliminary-lessons/

replies.title: Re: Anxiety and Panic
replies.content:

Not only that, but also scores high on Anticipation, Premonitions (of death and other misfortunes), and Anxiety. Unless of course he prescribed it as a cure for the panic attacks!!!

In general, metallic remedies are nasty twisted critters (heavy metal poisoning is no walk in the park, and "similar cures similar"). I'd recommend talking with you homeopath as soon as possible and either adjusting the dose or using a different remedy altogether.

Also, try not mixing it with other mind altering substances.

comments.title: Searching spiritual guide
comments.content:
Hello, JMG.

I threw a divination asking for a spiritual guide. Not sure what I was expecting, maybe my inner wyvern, maybe some christian saint. However, the vision was very clearly pointing towards Neptune. At first I didn't know what to do about that. Although I live in a coastal city, I don't dwell too much near the sea. It was puzzling me. Then a wild beast going to a mountain, then a rock on the sea, then a sail boat, then the mountain and the rock again. The wild beast has to be me, confounded and dangerous.

If the sea is the inner world, then this three faceted unity first lives with all his facets in the same realm, the mountain. Then one of the facets dive into the sea, or just in the surface, to be resurfaced again. And repeats.
This means that only one of my facets has to explore the inner world while the other two remain where they are. Also, this is a process that must be repeated: explore the inner world, be grounded, explore again.
I remember to have thought the following:
Three facets in three places, three times three, goes nine.

Here, when I was writing my findings in the journal app in my smartphone, my phone went nuts and I lost control of it for a few minutes. When I recovered control, it was exactly 9:33. Well, it could have been a coincidence, but that was striking, so I wonder if the universe wanted me to pay attention to it. So noted, nine is three times three.

Other than sharing my experience, I would like to ask how can I explore my inner world in a safe way.

comments.title: Alcohol and Spiritual Practice
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

For those engaging in regular spiritual and/or magical practices, what is your general recommendation with regard to alcohol consumption? Thank you!

Murky Unkempt Frog

replies.title: Re: Alcohol and Spiritual Practice
replies.content:
A little social drinking now and again isn't going to interfere with magical practice, nor is a beer or two on a hot day. Don't do magical work when you're drunk, and don't use alcohol to run away from your stress or your depression; other than that, ethanol is just another sugar.

One of the main divisions in spiritual traditions is between those traditions that aim for perfection and those that aim for wholeness. Perfection-centered paths are all into forbidding this and excluding that; it seems to work for them, but it's not my glass of stout! Wholeness-centered paths focus instead on balance -- and that includes a balance between your enjoyments and your spiritual work. "Nothing too much!" was the advice of the old Greek mysteries, and it's worth following today, too.

1) Having the feet flat on the ground allows contact with telluric energies. That's useful for discursive meditation and for Western occult spirituality generally. Zazen, and other Asian modes of meditation, have their own requirements and should be practiced in whatever posture your teacher suggests.

2) Yes. Most of the great mages of the 20th century smoked tobacco.

replies.content:
to offer my own experience as regards question 2 -

I smoke cigarettes on and off these days. When I do smoke, I smoke somewhere between half a pack and a pack per day. The times when I stop, after I've made it through the grouchy/foggy first few days, are when my Astral vision (i.e. perceiving LVX in rituals, vividness and sharpness of Pentagrams etc) is at its best. Though I suspect this has nothing to do with tobacco per se and much more to do with all the other chemicals in the tobacco and the cigarettes' filters. I also think that whether or not I have a smartphone has much to do with this. When I was first taking up Magick I got some of my most vivid results from ritual, and at that time I wasn't a smoker, but I also had a little flip phone. God, do I miss that thing.
The two members of the A.'.A.'. lineage to which I belong who I know personally are both Adepts and are both smokers, but it may be worth noting that they seem to smoke hand-rolled cigarettes from a brand which doesn't spray or otherwise cut their cigarette tobacco with chemicals.

Also, when I smoke a pipe it doesn't seem to interfere nearly so much, and it seems like the great mages of the past who used tobacco with whom I'm familiar all tended more toward the pipe than cigarettes themselves. It also, in addition to being easier on the lungs, looks much cooler.

comments.title: Christian Rosenkreuz
comments.content: Do you happen to know if anyone has had experience with Christian Rosenkreuz as a sort of "saint" or even deity that answers prayers, intercedes for devotees, etc.?

replies.title: Re: Christian Rosenkreuz
replies.content: The phrase you want is "inner plane adept." That's the standard term in occult circles for the equivalent of a saint -- that is to say, a human being who is now out of incarnation but remains very much an active, powerful presence in the world. Yes, CRC is one of those, and quite a few people have worked with him.

replies.title: Re: Christian Rosenkreuz
replies.content: Very interesting.  Thanks!

Though I suppose lighting a candle in his honor might also be construed as an offering of sorts?

Anyway, thanks again. For some reason, I'm now quite interested in learning more about these inner plane adepts. Might be my Catholic background showing.

replies.title: Re: Christian Rosenkreuz
replies.content: I've never heard of anyone burning a candle to an inner plane adept, though no doubt it's been done somewhere. No, the usual approach is to study the relevant texts -- in this case the Fama Fraternitatis and The Chymical Wedding -- meditate on them, and then use active imagination aka scrying in the spirit vision to reach out to him and see what response you get.
